input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, featuring lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements. It operates within the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) domain of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), characterized by resource-constrained electronic control units (ECUs) requiring computation offloading for optimal performance.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three key services: (1) Data Monitoring, which includes real-time visualization of vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks via a user interface (UI) for data viewing, saving, and report generation; (2) Computation Offloading, where the ACC system's lead vehicle detection, speed computation based on distance, and actuation commands are offloaded from the vehicle's ECUs to the DT; (3) Remote Management, enabling operators to remotely enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and send emergency braking commands through the UI, while developers use data collection for troubleshooting during system development.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) middleware, NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputer, Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera, with each functional element implemented as ROS2 packages and communication over Wi-Fi network.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for coordination between devices to enable computation offloading, adhering to safety-critical vehicular standards where stricter synchronization is mandated, with hardware limitations necessitating this specific threshold.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system comprises three distinct entities (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity), each deployed on separate hardware components. The User Interface Functional Entity (FE) is split into two subcomponents (3D Visualization and Control Panel), while the Data Storage component lacks a naturally aligned FE. Software components are mapped to FEs as individual ROS2 packages, each containing one or more ROS2 nodes organized by the ISO 23247 RA entity-based structure.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows ISO 23247 RA with deviations, particularly the Physical Twin Entity hosting general automotive functionality (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking) instead of only resource-specific functionality as in the standard. All software components are implemented as ROS2 packages with ROS2 nodes, deployed on distinct hardware, reflecting a significant departure from ISO 23247 RA impacting functionality ownership and engineering processes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Data Storage gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database. Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN and tracks using a Kalman filter.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Vedder Electronic Speed Controller, Motor, and Steering Servo are physical actuators. Actuation Functional Entity converts desired speed into motor voltage commands.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR and Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera provide sensing. Data Collection collects object tracks and ego vehicle state.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from physical sensors including LiDAR point cloud, vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path is transmitted over Wi-Fi using UDP for inter-entity communication. Raw point cloud data is processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, while the Data Translation component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions. Internal communication between software components occurs via ROS2, with real-time data monitoring displayed in the 3D Visualization.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands such as desired speed and emergency braking are generated by the ACC Controlling FE and transmitted through Data Translation. The Actuation FE converts desired speed into motor voltage commands for vehicle actuation. Emergency braking commands trigger immediate physical responses. Command validation, authentication, and feedback mechanisms are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Internal communication uses ROS2 protocol, while inter-entity communication operates over Wi-Fi using UDP in the transport layer. The system enforces a maximum network latency of 100 ms as specified in Requirement R7. Bandwidth requirements, redundancy mechanisms, and security protocols are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Ego vehicle state data is stored on external devices separate from the vehicle hardware. Each entity (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) is deployed on distinct hardware components. Specific hosting platforms (cloud, edge, on-premise) and scalability mechanisms are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system generates insights through Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management services as defined in Section III.A. Analytical capabilities include Report Generation and Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) components specified in Table I. User interaction for decision-making is enabled via a 3D Visualization interface and Control Panel, providing real-time monitoring and control functionalities.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Adherence to ISO 23247:2021 Digital Twin framework standard for manufacturing, Part 2 (Reference Architecture), which structures the Digital Twin into entities: Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity, each composed of Functional Entities (FEs). The standard specifies information exchange protocols and notes OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as a possible implementation choice for FEs, though it does not prescribe specific implementation methods for FEs.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

For Computation Offloading, it accurately describes the process: 'offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT' which 'determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to the vehicle'. For Remote Management, it correctly lists the capabilities: 'enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command' and notes the user types (operators and developers). The extraction is highly specific and grounded in the text.

The description lists the specific hardware, software SDKs, and middleware detailed in Section III.B and IV.B. It correctly notes the implementation method using ROS2 packages and the communication network. Supported by quotes: 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', 'NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputer', 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera', 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', and 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
